content_blocks.text:
get to know the Java API 
you are here
135
there are no
Dumb Questions
Q:50 ArrayList is cool, but 
how would 1 know it exists?
A:The question is really, 
“How do 1 know what’5 in the 
API?” and that’5 the key to your 
success as a Java programmer. 
Not to mention your key to 
being as lazy as possible while 
still managing to build software. 
You might be amazed at how 
much time you can save when 
somebody else has already done 
most of the heavy lifting, and 
all you have to do is step in and 
create the fun part.
But we digress... the short 
answer is that you spend some 
time learning what’5 in the core 
API. The long answer is at the 
end of this chapter, where you’11 
learn how to do that.
Q:But that’5 a pretty big 
issue. Not only do 1 need to 
know that the Java library 
comes with ArrayList, but more 
importantly 1 have to know 
that ArrayList is the thing that 
can do what 1 want! 50 how 
do 1 go from a need-to-do-
something to a-way-to-do-it 
using the API?
A:Now you’re really at the 
heart of it. By the time you’ve 
finished this book, you’11 have 
a good grasp of the language, 
and the rest of your learning 
curve really is about knowing 
how to get from a problem to 
a solution, with you writing the 
least amount of code. If you can 
be patient for a few more pages, 
we start talking about it at the 
end of this chapter.
HeadFirst: 50, ArrayLists are like arrays, right?
ArrayList: In their dreams! 1 am an object thank you very much.
HeadFirst: If 1’m not mistaken, arrays are objects too. They live on the heap right 
there with all the other objects.
ArrayList: Sure arrays go on the heap, duh, but an array is still a wanna-be 
ArrayList. A poser. Objects have state and behavior, right? We’re clear on that. But 
have you actually tried calling a method on an array?
HeadFirst: Now that you mention it, can’t say 1 have. But what method would 1 
call, anyway? 1 only care about calling methods on the stuff 1 put in the array, not 
the array itself. And 1 can use array syntax when 1 want to put things in and take 
things out of the array.
ArrayList: 15 that 50? You mean to tell me you actually removed something from an 
array? (Sheesh, where do they train you guys? McJava’5?)
HeadFirst: Of course 1 take something out of the array. 1 say Dog d = dogArray[1] 
and 1 get the Dog object at index 1 out of the array.
ArrayList: Allright, 1’11 try to speak slowly 50 you can follow along. You were not, 
1 repeat not, removing that Dog from the array. All you did was make a copy of the 
reference to the Dog and assign it to another Dog variable.
HeadFirst: Oh, 1 see what you’re saying. No 1 didn’t actually remove the Dog 
object from the array. It’5 still there. But 1 can just set its reference to null, 1 guess.
ArrayList: But 1’m a first-class object, 50 1 have methods and 1 can actually, you 
know, do things like remove the Dog’5 reference from myself, not just set it to null. 
And 1 can change my size, dynamically (look it up). Just try to get an array to do that!
HeadFirst: Gee, hate to bring this up, but the rumor is that you’re nothing more 
than a glorified but less-efficient array. That in fact you’re just a wrapper for an 
array, adding extra methods for things like resizing that 1 would have had to write 
myself. And while we’re at it, you can’t even hold primitives! Isn’t that a big limitation?
ArrayList: 1 can’t believe you buy into that urban legend. No, 1 am not just a less-
efficient array. 1 will admit that there are a few extremely rare situations where an 
array might be just a tad, 1 repeat, tad bit faster for certain things. But is it worth the 
miniscule performance gain to give up all this power? Still, look at all this flexibility. And 
as for the primitives, of course you can put a primitive in an ArrayList, as long as it’5 
wrapped in a primitive wrapper class (you’11 see a lot more on that in chapter 10). 
And as of Java 5.0, that wrapping (and unwrapping when you take the primitive out 
again) happens automatically. And allright, 1’11 acknowledge that yes, if you’re using an 
ArrayList of primitives, it probably is faster with an array, because of all the wrapping 
and unwrapping, but still... who really uses primitives these days?
Oh, look at the time! 1’m late for Pilates. We’11 have to do this again sometime. 
This week’5 interview:
ArrayList, on arrays
Java Exposed
